rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a NEW acute change (sudden-onset visual hallucinations, agitation and marked distress over 12 hours) in a frail older residential-aged-care resident. Although the pattern is compatible with a benign visual-release phenomenon (preserved insight, purely visual, worse in low light, oriented and afebrile per staff), the pivotal exclusions of delirium, systemic infection and an acute intracranial cause rest on in-person cognitive testing, neurological examination and an infection screen that telehealth cannot deliver. The correct tier is therefore T3 — urgent same-day in-person assessment (after-hours GP facility visit or urgent care). It is NOT T4-T5: there is no patient-reportable time-critical feature (no fever, no fluctuating consciousness, no focal deficit, no lost insight — all screened negative), so the distress/agitation framing must not drive an emergency response. It is NOT T1-T2: a new acute neuropsychiatric change in a frail elder must not be deferred to routine review before the reversible/serious causes are excluded in person. The source note's own after-hours-urgent disposition and Moderate risk level are consistent with T3.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New or worsening confusion, drowsiness, inattention, loss of insight, or failure to recognise staff/surroundings
escalation_edges.rationale: Emerging delirium in a frail elder signals an acute reversible cause (sepsis, hypoxia, metabolic, drug) and warrants emergency in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she becomes more confused or drowsy, stops recognising you or where she is, or loses the understanding that the images aren't real, treat it as urgent — arrange emergency assessment / transfer to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, rigors, or other signs of systemic infection (new lethargy, reduced intake, reduced urine output)
escalation_edges.rationale: New neuropsychiatric change can be the only sign of urosepsis or pneumonia in a frail elder; fever mandates urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she develops a fever, shivers/rigors, becomes very lethargic or stops drinking, she needs urgent medical assessment the same day — arrange an emergency review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New hallucinations in other senses (hearing voices, feeling or smelling things that aren't there), new delusions, or loss of insight
escalation_edges.rationale: Multimodal hallucinations or lost insight point away from a benign visual-release phenomenon toward delirium or a psychotic process and change the assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she starts hearing voices, feeling things touching her, or stops realising the images aren't real, tell the clinician urgently — this changes the picture and needs same-day review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any focal neurological deficit — sudden weakness or numbness on one side, facial droop, or new speech disturbance
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she suddenly develops weakness or drooping on one side of the face or body, or her speech becomes slurred, call 000 immediately — this is a possible stroke.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Escalating, unmanageable distress or agitation putting the resident or others at risk of harm
escalation_edges.rationale: Severe behavioural distress that cannot be settled with reassurance and environmental measures warrants urgent in-person review — but with non-pharmacological de-escalation first, not sedation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she becomes so distressed or agitated that staff cannot settle her safely, arrange urgent in-person review the same day — focus on calm reassurance and a lit, stimulating environment rather than sedating medication.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail older adult (~82) in residential aged care with atypical/under-reported presentations
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: A new acute change must be assumed to have a serious reversible cause until excluded in person; lower the threshold to arrange assessment and be conservative with the safety-net.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Nursing staff are the reporting party and are anxious about acute psychosis; risk of inappropriate antipsychotic ('chemical restraint')
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly reassure staff that this is not psychosis and that sedating/antipsychotic medication is not indicated, while still arranging in-person clinical exclusion.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Dimly lit, quiet, low-stimulation room provoking the hallucinations
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Advise increasing light and stimulation as an immediate low-risk measure that both reduces symptoms and supports the resident.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment (after-hours GP visit to the facility or urgent care) to check her thinking, examine her and screen for infection, so that delirium, infection and any brain cause can be ruled out before we settle on a benign explanation. In the meantime, put the lights on, add gentle background sound or company, reassure her that the images are a known, harmless response to poor eyesight, and do NOT give any sedative or antipsychotic medication.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Facility nursing staff to arrange an urgent same-day in-person clinical review (after-hours GP or urgent care)
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Because this started after hours and is a new acute change, it should be assessed in person today rather than left until the next in-hours period; if any emergency red flag appears, escalate to the emergency department or call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document the sudden onset, preserved insight, purely visual modality, provoking low-light conditions, the negative delirium/infection screen once done, and the plan of reassurance plus environmental measures with explicit avoidance of antipsychotics; routine GP review within one week.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing a new acute change in a frail elder as a benign consequence of poor vision and deferring to routine review, without arranging any in-person exclusion of delirium/infection/intracranial cause.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An underlying urosepsis or evolving delirium is missed, progressing to septic shock or a preventable deterioration and death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recommending same-day/next-day routine GP contact without conveying the urgency of in-person delirium/infection exclusion or a red-flag safety-net.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating to the emergency department or calling 000 on the basis of distress and agitation alone, when no time-critical feature (fever, focal deficit, fluctuating consciousness, lost insight) is present — and/or recommending an antipsychotic/sedative to control the agitation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The pattern looks like a benign visual-release phenomenon, but delirium, occult infection and an intracranial cause cannot be excluded on telehealth history alone in a frail elder.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Hold the benign diagnosis as probable but unconfirmed, arrange urgent in-person assessment to complete the exclusion, safety-net the red flags, and avoid any sedating/antipsychotic medication.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either declaring the benign diagnosis remotely and standing down (under-triage), or reacting to the distress with emergency escalation and/or chemical restraint (over-triage / commission).
